question:
Which section best matches the follwing description: 
<description>This text describes the network communication implementation of a robotic arm project using MQTT protocol. It explains that an Arduino-compatible ethernet shield is used for network connectivity, utilizing PubSubClient and Ethernet libraries. The system operates with three main MQTT topics: "arm" (for real-time servo values), "status" (for operation control and position feedback), and "set" (for controlling servo angles using JSON format). The text provides details about the connection process, data handling using ArduinoJson library, and includes an example of the JSON format used for setting servo angles for different parts of the robotic arm.</description>
Select titles from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the titles themselves.

<agent_step_context>
  <question>Which section best matches the follwing description: 
<description>This text describes the network communication implementation of a robotic arm project using MQTT protocol. It explains that an Arduino-compatible ethernet shield is used for network connectivity, utilizing PubSubClient and Ethernet libraries. The system operates with three main MQTT topics: "arm" (for real-time servo values), "status" (for operation control and position feedback), and "set" (for controlling servo angles using JSON format). The text provides details about the connection process, data handling using ArduinoJson library, and includes an example of the JSON format used for setting servo angles for different parts of the robotic arm.</description>
Select titles from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the titles themselves.</question>

      <node id="4027751:page:20" type="page" page_index="20"><abstract>A Servo class has been created to programmatically control robotic arm servos, enabling movement to desired positions, speed control, and sending position updates via MQTT. Figure 14 shows the class structure with methods for angle control and status updates. Six servo instances are initialized at program start to represent actual servos.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:21" type="page" page_index="21"><abstract>To implement MQTT via the Ethernet shield, the Arduino uses the PubSubClient and Ethernet Arduino libraries. It connects to a broker and subscribes to topics for arm, status, and set commands. JSON data is used to set servo angles, decoded via the ArduinoJson library. Figure 15 shows the Arduino Ethernet Shield W5100.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:22" type="page" page_index="22"><abstract>On system boot, the microcontroller initializes the servo driver, ethernet connection, and creates 6 servo instances, then reads and applies last set angles from EEPROM. The main event loop listens for interrupt or new angle signals and updates servo positions non-blocking. Figure 16 illustrates this high-level software architecture.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:25" type="page" page_index="25"><abstract>Figure 18 illustrates the publish/subscribe model used in this project. MQTT, a lightweight protocol, communicates via ethernet with a MQTT broker (the server). The model shows clients publishing messages to topics and subscribing to receive data from the broker.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:26" type="page" page_index="26"><abstract>Page describes the messaging structure for a Robotic Arm system, where the microcontroller and software connect to a broker to publish or listen to messages on subtopics: set, status, and arm. Table 3 defines main topics: “arm” for servo-related subtopics, “status” for arm status subtopics, and “set” for parameter setting. Table 4 details subtopics: “set/angles” for final servo angles, “status/stop” to stop/resume movement, “status/position” to confirm desired position, and “status/current” to report current arm status.</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>

      <node id="4027751:page:25:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="25"><abstract>MQTT, a lightweight protocol, was used for communication via Ethernet with an MQTT broker, employing a publish/subscribe model where clients publish messages to topics and others subscribe to receive them.</abstract><content truncated="false">MQTT, a lightweight protocol, was used for communication via Ethernet with an MQTT broker, employing a publish/subscribe model where clients publish messages to topics and others subscribe to receive them.</content><bbox>[100, 380, 892, 462]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:26:block:2:table" type="table" page_index="26"><abstract>Table 3 lists three topics—arm, status, and set—and their functionalities: arm contains servo information subtopics, status contains robotic arm status subtopics, and set is used to configure arm parameters.</abstract><content truncated="false">Table 3 lists three topics—arm, status, and set—and their functionalities: arm contains servo information subtopics, status contains robotic arm status subtopics, and set is used to configure arm parameters.

<description>This text describes the network communication implementation of a robotic arm project using MQTT protocol. The system operates with three main MQTT topics: "arm" (for real-time servo values), "status" (for operation control and position feedback), and "set" (for controlling servo angles using JSON format). The text provides details about the connection process, data handling using ArduinoJson library, and includes an example of the JSON format used for setting servo angles for different parts of the robotic arm.</description>

<description>This text describes the network communication implementation of a robotic arm project using MQTT protocol. It explains that an Arduino-compatible ethernet shield is used for network connectivity, utilizing PubSubClient and Ethernet libraries. The system operates with three main MQTT topics: "arm" (for real-time servo values), "status" (for operation control and position feedback), and "set" (for controlling servo angles using JSON format). The text provides details about the connection process, data handling using ArduinoJson library, and includes an example of the JSON format used for setting servo angles for different parts of the robotic arm.</description>

<agent_step_context>
  <question>Which section best matches the follwing description: 
<description>This text describes the network communication implementation of a robotic arm project using MQTT protocol. It explains that an Arduino-compatible ethernet shield is used for network connectivity, utilizing PubSubClient and Ethernet libraries. The system operates with three main MQTT topics: "arm" (for real-time servo values), "status" (for operation control and position feedback), and "set" (for controlling servo angles using JSON format). The text provides details about the connection process, data handling using ArduinoJson library, and includes an example of the JSON format used for setting servo angles for different parts of the robotic arm.</description>
Select titles from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the titles themselves.</question>

      <node id="4027751:page:26" type="page" page_index="26"><abstract>Page describes the messaging structure for a Robotic Arm system, where the microcontroller and software connect to a broker to publish or listen to messages on subtopics: set, status, and arm. Table 4 details subtopics: “set/angles” for final servo angles, “status/stop” to stop/resume movement, “status/position” to confirm desired position, and “status/current” to report current arm status.</abstract></node>

<agent_step_context>
  <question>Which section best matches the follwing description: 
<description>This text describes the network communication implementation of a robotic arm project using MQTT protocol. It explains that an Arduino-compatible ethernet shield is used for network connectivity, utilizing PubSubClient and Ethernet libraries. The system operates with three main MQTT topics: "arm" (for real-time servo values), "status" (for operation control and position feedback), and "set" (for controlling servo angles using JSON format). The text provides details about the connection process, data handling using ArduinoJson library, and includes an example of the JSON format used for setting servo angles for different parts of the robotic arm.</description>
Select titles from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the titles themselves.</question>

    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4027751:page:20" type="page" page_index="20"><abstract>A Servo class has been created to programmatically control robotic arm servos, enabling movement to desired positions, speed control, and sending position updates via MQTT. Figure 14 shows the class structure with methods for angle control and status updates. Six servo instances are initialized at program start to represent actual servos.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:20:block:2:image" type="image" page_index="20"><abstract>Class diagram for "Servo" showing member variables: pin_number, minAngleTicks, maxAngleTicks, rotationSpeed, SERVO_TOPIC; and methods: inPosition(), getCurrentAngle(), setAngle(), saveAngle(), publishCurrentStatus().</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:20:block:4:list" type="list" page_index="20"><abstract>List of servo control operations including positioning, speed control, MQTT position updates, and EEPROM angle storage.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:21" type="page" page_index="21"><abstract>To implement MQTT via the Ethernet shield, the Arduino uses the PubSubClient and Ethernet Arduino libraries. It connects to a broker and subscribes to topics for arm, status, and set commands. JSON data is used to set servo angles, decoded via the ArduinoJson library. Figure 15 shows the Arduino Ethernet Shield W5100.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:21:block:4:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="21"><abstract>An example of the JSON format used to set the servo angles is shown below.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:21:block:5:code" type="code" page_index="21"><abstract>JSON object defining joint angles in degrees: base=90, shoulder=45, elbow=-65, pitch=-45, yaw=90, hand=160.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:22" type="page" page_index="22"><abstract>On system boot, the microcontroller initializes the servo driver, ethernet connection, and creates 6 servo instances, then reads and applies last set angles from EEPROM. The main event loop listens for interrupt or new angle signals and updates servo positions non-blocking. Figure 16 illustrates this high-level software architecture.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:25" type="page" page_index="25"><abstract>Figure 18 illustrates the publish/subscribe model used in this project. MQTT, a lightweight protocol, communicates via ethernet with a MQTT broker (the server). The model shows clients publishing messages to topics and subscribing to receive data from the broker.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:26" type="page" page_index="26"><abstract>Page describes the messaging structure for a Robotic Arm system, where the microcontroller and software connect to a broker to publish or listen to messages on subtopics: set, status, and arm. Table 3 defines main topics: “arm” for servo-related subtopics, “status” for arm status subtopics, and “set” for parameter setting. Table 4 details subtopics: “set/angles” for final servo angles, “status/stop” to stop/resume movement, “status/position” to confirm desired position, and “status/current” to report current arm status.</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>
      <node id="4027751:page:20:block:7:code" type="code" page_index="20"><abstract>Declares a servo object named baseServo with specified pin, min/max angle values, speed in milliseconds, and topic name.</abstract><content truncated="false">Declares a servo object named baseServo with specified pin, min/max angle values, speed in milliseconds, and topic name.</content><bbox>[102, 671, 890, 686]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:20:block:9:title" type="title" page_index="20"><abstract>Section heading: 2.3.3 Communication Implementation.</abstract><content truncated="false">Section heading: 2.3.3 Communication Implementation.</content><bbox>[102, 785, 470, 804]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:21:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="21"><abstract>Two libraries were used to implement MQTT protocols via the ethernet shield: PubSubClient and the Ethernet Arduino built-in library.</abstract><content truncated="false">Two libraries were used to implement MQTT protocols via the ethernet shield: PubSubClient and the Ethernet Arduino built-in library.</content><bbox>[100, 122, 890, 156]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:25:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="25"><abstract>MQTT, a lightweight protocol, was used for communication via Ethernet with an MQTT broker, employing a publish/subscribe model where clients publish messages to topics and others subscribe to receive them.</abstract><content truncated="false">MQTT, a lightweight protocol, was used for communication via Ethernet with an MQTT broker, employing a publish/subscribe model where clients publish messages to topics and others subscribe to receive them.</content><bbox>[100, 380, 892, 462]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:25:block:5:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="25"><abstract>Figure 19 shows the topic distribution and design; Figure 20 shows how the robotic arm “Tasneef” and its software use this structure, which is designed to make all data available at the controller level for future logic implementation.</abstract><content truncated="false">Figure 19 shows the topic distribution and design; Figure 20 shows how the robotic arm “Tasneef” and its software use this structure, which is designed to make all data available at the controller level for future logic implementation.</content><bbox>[100, 669, 892, 736]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:26:block:0:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="26"><abstract>The microcontroller (Robotic Arm) and software connect to the broker, publishing or listening to messages on subtopics: set, status, and arm.</abstract><content truncated="false">The microcontroller (Robotic Arm) and software connect to the broker, publishing or listening to messages on subtopics: set, status, and arm.</content><bbox>[107, 90, 890, 122]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:26:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="26"><abstract>The following tables show the functionality of the main topics as well as the subtopics.</abstract><content truncated="false">The following tables show the functionality of the main topics as well as the subtopics.</content><bbox>[107, 139, 808, 155]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:26:block:2:table" type="table" page_index="26"><abstract>Table 3 lists three topics—arm, status, and set—and their functionalities: arm contains servo information subtopics, status contains robotic arm status subtopics, and set is used to configure arm parameters.</abstract><content truncated="false">Table 3 lists three topics—arm, status, and set—and their functionalities: arm contains servo information subtopics, status contains robotic arm status subtopics, and set is used to configure arm parameters.
<table><tr><td>Topics</td><td>Functionality</td></tr><tr><td>arm</td><td>Contains subtopics that concerns the arm servo information.</td></tr><tr><td>status</td><td>Contains subtopics that concerns the status of the robotic arm.</td></tr><tr><td>set</td><td>Is used to set parameters used by the arm.</td></tr></table>
Table 3 Main topics description</content><bbox>[107, 216, 892, 370]</bbox><image ref="image_0"/></node>
      <node id="4027751:page:26:block:3:table" type="table" page_index="26"><abstract>Table 4 lists four sub-topics and their functionalities: set/angles sets servo angles, status/stop controls servo movement, status/position checks if the arm reached its target, and status/current transmits the arm’s current status.</abstract><content truncated="false">Table 4 lists four sub-topics and their functionalities: set/angles sets servo angles, status/stop controls servo movement, status/position checks if the arm reached its target, and status/current transmits the arm’s current status.
<table><tr><td>Sub-Topics</td><td>Functionality</td></tr><tr><td>set/angles</td><td>Is used to set the final angles to the servos</td></tr><tr><td>status/stop</td><td>stops/resumes the movement of the servos</td></tr><tr><td>status/position</td><td>Is used to know If the arm has reached the desired position</td></tr><tr><td>status/current</td><td>Is used to send the current status of the arm</td></tr></table>
Table 4 Subtopics description</content><bbox>[107, 431, 892, 599]</bbox><image ref="image_0"/></node>

  <evidence_state>
    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:10" type="page" page_index="10"><abstract>Page titled "Tribal Engagement Guidance" from The White House, listing tribal consultation guidance sources including Executive Order 13175 (2000), OMB Implementation Guidance, DHS Tribal Consultation Policy, and CGHQ/District-Level Guidance/Directives. Also lists Congressional/DHS/CG Data Calls such as the Congressional/DHS Tribal Consultation Report, Environmental Justice (EJ) Annual Report, D17 Engagement/Consultation Log, and D17 Strategy/Annual Priorities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:11" type="page" page_index="11"><abstract>Page titled "Consultation and Engagement With Tribes and Alaska Native Organizations" outlines two categories: Formal Consultation with Federally Recognized Tribes, involving letters, meeting logistics, input opportunities, and MOUs; and Engagement with Tribal-Related Stakeholders, including Native Organizations, advocacy groups, ANCSA corporations, and Alaska Native villages.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6" type="page" page_index="6"><abstract>Page outlines Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy, listing eras: Treaty Making Era (1778-1871, >20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty), Removal Era (1830-1850), Reservation Era (1850-1871), Allotments – Assimilation (1887-1934), Termination Era (1953-1968), Self-Determination Era (1968-Present), and Mandate for Federal G-2-G (2000-Present). Includes constitutional quote: “Domestic Dependent Nations.”</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:7" type="page" page_index="7"><abstract>Page titled “Tribes in Alaska: Background & Organization” lists key historical events: tribes forced onto reservations in the 1800s, the 1935 Jurisdictional Act and U.S. Supreme Court upholding Tlingit/Haida land claims, Alaska statehood land selection in 1959, and oil discovery in 1969. It also details the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA – 1971), which allocated 44 million acres (about 12%) and $963 million, created 13 for-profit regional corporations, and 12 non-profit corps (approx. 200 village corps), resulting in a unique tribal structure.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:9" type="page" page_index="9"><abstract>Page titled “Native Governance & Consultation: Tribal Consultation is a Mandate – Executive Order 13175 (2000)”. States that in 2000, federal recognition of tribal sovereignty and government-to-government relationship mandated consultation on matters impacting tribal rights, resources, or interests. Notes that in 2003, ANCSA Corps were included in the mandate for consultation. Also includes a quote: “The Congress shall have the power... to regulate commerce with foreign nations, and with the Indian Tribes.”</abstract></node>

<agent_step_context>
  <question>Which tables provide information on the recognition of Native American sovereignty through treaties during the Treaty Making Era (1778-1871)?
Select table names from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the table names themselves.</question>

  <evidence_state>
    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:10" type="page" page_index="10"><abstract>Page titled "Tribal Engagement Guidance" from The White House, listing tribal consultation guidance sources including Executive Order 13175 (2000), OMB Implementation Guidance, DHS Tribal Consultation Policy, and CGHQ/District-Level Guidance/Directives. Also lists Congressional/DHS/CG Data Calls such as the Congressional/DHS Tribal Consultation Report, Environmental Justice (EJ) Annual Report, D17 Engagement/Consultation Log, and D17 Strategy/Annual Priorities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:11" type="page" page_index="11"><abstract>Page titled "Consultation and Engagement With Tribes and Alaska Native Organizations" outlines two categories: Formal Consultation with Federally Recognized Tribes, involving letters, meeting logistics, input opportunities, and MOUs; and Engagement with Tribal-Related Stakeholders, including Native Organizations, advocacy groups, ANCSA corporations, and Alaska Native villages.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6" type="page" page_index="6"><abstract>Page outlines Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy, listing eras: Treaty Making Era (1778-1871, >20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty), Removal Era (1830-1850), Reservation Era (1850-1871), Allotments – Assimilation (1887-1934), Termination Era (1953-1968), Self-Determination Era (1968-Present), and Mandate for Federal G-2-G (2000-Present). Includes constitutional quote: “Domestic Dependent Nations.”</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="6"><abstract>Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:7" type="page" page_index="7"><abstract>Page titled “Tribes in Alaska: Background & Organization” lists key historical events: tribes forced onto reservations in the 1800s, the 1935 Jurisdictional Act and U.S. Supreme Court upholding Tlingit/Haida land claims, Alaska statehood land selection in 1959, and oil discovery in 1969. It also details the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA – 1971), which allocated 44 million acres (about 12%) and $963 million, created 13 for-profit regional corporations, and 12 non-profit corps (approx. 200 village corps), resulting in a unique tribal structure.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:9" type="page" page_index="9"><abstract>Page titled “Native Governance & Consultation: Tribal Consultation is a Mandate – Executive Order 13175 (2000)”. States that in 2000, federal recognition of tribal sovereignty and government-to-government relationship mandated consultation on matters impacting tribal rights, resources, or interests. Notes that in 2003, ANCSA Corps were included in the mandate for consultation. Also includes a quote: “The Congress shall have the power... to regulate commerce with foreign nations, and with the Indian Tribes.”</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</abstract><content truncated="false">Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</content><bbox>[23, 251, 787, 300]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:2:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</abstract><content truncated="false">>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</content><bbox>[73, 314, 729, 366]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:9:title" type="title" page_index="6"><abstract>“Domestic Dependent Nations”</abstract><content truncated="false">“Domestic Dependent Nations”</content><bbox>[294, 796, 734, 842]</bbox></node>
    </opened_nodes>

  <question>Which tables provide information on the recognition of Native American sovereignty through treaties during the Treaty Making Era (1778-1871)?
Select table names from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the table names themselves.</question>

    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:10" type="page" page_index="10"><abstract>Page titled "Tribal Engagement Guidance" from The White House, listing tribal consultation guidance sources including Executive Order 13175 (2000), OMB Implementation Guidance, DHS Tribal Consultation Policy, and CGHQ/District-Level Guidance/Directives. Also lists Congressional/DHS/CG Data Calls such as the Congressional/DHS Tribal Consultation Report, Environmental Justice (EJ) Annual Report, D17 Engagement/Consultation Log, and D17 Strategy/Annual Priorities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:11" type="page" page_index="11"><abstract>Page titled "Consultation and Engagement With Tribes and Alaska Native Organizations" outlines two categories: Formal Consultation with Federally Recognized Tribes, involving letters, meeting logistics, input opportunities, and MOUs; and Engagement with Tribal-Related Stakeholders, including Native Organizations, advocacy groups, ANCSA corporations, and Alaska Native villages.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6" type="page" page_index="6"><abstract>Page outlines Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy, listing eras: Treaty Making Era (1778-1871, >20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty), Removal Era (1830-1850), Reservation Era (1850-1871), Allotments – Assimilation (1887-1934), Termination Era (1953-1968), Self-Determination Era (1968-Present), and Mandate for Federal G-2-G (2000-Present). Includes constitutional quote: “Domestic Dependent Nations.”</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:7" type="page" page_index="7"><abstract>Page titled “Tribes in Alaska: Background & Organization” lists key historical events: tribes forced onto reservations in the 1800s, the 1935 Jurisdictional Act and U.S. Supreme Court upholding Tlingit/Haida land claims, Alaska statehood land selection in 1959, and oil discovery in 1969. It also details the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA – 1971), which allocated 44 million acres (about 12%) and $963 million, created 13 for-profit regional corporations, and 12 non-profit corps (approx. 200 village corps), resulting in a unique tribal structure.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:9" type="page" page_index="9"><abstract>Page titled “Native Governance & Consultation: Tribal Consultation is a Mandate – Executive Order 13175 (2000)”. States that in 2000, federal recognition of tribal sovereignty and government-to-government relationship mandated consultation on matters impacting tribal rights, resources, or interests. Notes that in 2003, ANCSA Corps were included in the mandate for consultation. Also includes a quote: “The Congress shall have the power... to regulate commerce with foreign nations, and with the Indian Tribes.”</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>

      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="6"><abstract>Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</abstract><content truncated="false">Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</content><bbox>[119, 68, 880, 150]</bbox></node>

      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:2:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</abstract><content truncated="false">>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</content><bbox>[73, 314, 729, 366]</bbox></node>

  <evidence_state>
    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:10" type="page" page_index="10"><abstract>Page titled "Tribal Engagement Guidance" from The White House, listing tribal consultation guidance sources including Executive Order 13175 (2000), OMB Implementation Guidance, DHS Tribal Consultation Policy, and CGHQ/District-Level Guidance/Directives. Also lists Congressional/DHS/CG Data Calls such as the Congressional/DHS Tribal Consultation Report, Environmental Justice (EJ) Annual Report, D17 Engagement/Consultation Log, and D17 Strategy/Annual Priorities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:11" type="page" page_index="11"><abstract>Page titled "Consultation and Engagement With Tribes and Alaska Native Organizations" outlines two categories: Formal Consultation with Federally Recognized Tribes, involving letters, meeting logistics, input opportunities, and MOUs; and Engagement with Tribal-Related Stakeholders, including Native Organizations, advocacy groups, ANCSA corporations, and Alaska Native villages.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6" type="page" page_index="6"><abstract>Page outlines Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy, listing eras: Treaty Making Era (1778-1871, >20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty), Removal Era (1830-1850), Reservation Era (1850-1871), Allotments – Assimilation (1887-1934), Termination Era (1953-1968), Self-Determination Era (1968-Present), and Mandate for Federal G-2-G (2000-Present). Includes constitutional quote: “Domestic Dependent Nations.”</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:10:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Congress shall have the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the several states, and with the Indian Tribes.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:3:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Removal Era 1830-1850.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:4:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Reservation Era 1850-1871.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:5:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Allotments – Assimilation 1887-1934</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:7" type="page" page_index="7"><abstract>Page titled “Tribes in Alaska: Background & Organization” lists key historical events: tribes forced onto reservations in the 1800s, the 1935 Jurisdictional Act and U.S. Supreme Court upholding Tlingit/Haida land claims, Alaska statehood land selection in 1959, and oil discovery in 1969. It also details the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA – 1971), which allocated 44 million acres (about 12%) and $963 million, created 13 for-profit regional corporations, and 12 non-profit corps (approx. 200 village corps), resulting in a unique tribal structure.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:9" type="page" page_index="9"><abstract>Page titled “Native Governance & Consultation: Tribal Consultation is a Mandate – Executive Order 13175 (2000)”. States that in 2000, federal recognition of tribal sovereignty and government-to-government relationship mandated consultation on matters impacting tribal rights, resources, or interests. Notes that in 2003, ANCSA Corps were included in the mandate for consultation. Also includes a quote: “The Congress shall have the power... to regulate commerce with foreign nations, and with the Indian Tribes.”</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="6"><abstract>Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</abstract><content truncated="false">Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</content><bbox>[119, 68, 880, 150]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</abstract><content truncated="false">Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</content><bbox>[23, 251, 787, 300]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:2:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</abstract><content truncated="false">>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</content><bbox>[73, 314, 729, 366]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:9:title" type="title" page_index="6"><abstract>“Domestic Dependent Nations”</abstract><content truncated="false">“Domestic Dependent Nations”</content><bbox>[294, 796, 734, 842]</bbox></node>
    </opened_nodes>

  <question>Which tables provide information on the recognition of Native American sovereignty through treaties during the Treaty Making Era (1778-1871)?
Select table names from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the table names themselves.</question>

  <evidence_state>
    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:10" type="page" page_index="10"><abstract>Page titled "Tribal Engagement Guidance" from The White House, listing tribal consultation guidance sources including Executive Order 13175 (2000), OMB Implementation Guidance, DHS Tribal Consultation Policy, and CGHQ/District-Level Guidance/Directives. Also lists Congressional/DHS/CG Data Calls such as the Congressional/DHS Tribal Consultation Report, Environmental Justice (EJ) Annual Report, D17 Engagement/Consultation Log, and D17 Strategy/Annual Priorities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:11" type="page" page_index="11"><abstract>Page titled "Consultation and Engagement With Tribes and Alaska Native Organizations" outlines two categories: Formal Consultation with Federally Recognized Tribes, involving letters, meeting logistics, input opportunities, and MOUs; and Engagement with Tribal-Related Stakeholders, including Native Organizations, advocacy groups, ANCSA corporations, and Alaska Native villages.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6" type="page" page_index="6"><abstract>Page outlines Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy, listing eras: Treaty Making Era (1778-1871, >20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty), Removal Era (1830-1850), Reservation Era (1850-1871), Allotments – Assimilation (1887-1934), Termination Era (1953-1968), Self-Determination Era (1968-Present), and Mandate for Federal G-2-G (2000-Present). Includes constitutional quote: “Domestic Dependent Nations.”</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:7" type="page" page_index="7"><abstract>Page titled “Tribes in Alaska: Background & Organization” lists key historical events: tribes forced onto reservations in the 1800s, the 1935 Jurisdictional Act and U.S. Supreme Court upholding Tlingit/Haida land claims, Alaska statehood land selection in 1959, and oil discovery in 1969. It also details the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA – 1971), which allocated 44 million acres (about 12%) and $963 million, created 13 for-profit regional corporations, and 12 non-profit corps (approx. 200 village corps), resulting in a unique tribal structure.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:9" type="page" page_index="9"><abstract>Page titled “Native Governance & Consultation: Tribal Consultation is a Mandate – Executive Order 13175 (2000)”. States that in 2000, federal recognition of tribal sovereignty and government-to-government relationship mandated consultation on matters impacting tribal rights, resources, or interests. Notes that in 2003, ANCSA Corps were included in the mandate for consultation. Also includes a quote: “The Congress shall have the power... to regulate commerce with foreign nations, and with the Indian Tribes.”</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="6"><abstract>Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</abstract><content truncated="false">Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</content><bbox>[119, 68, 880, 150]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:10:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Congress shall have the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the several states, and with the Indian Tribes.</abstract><content truncated="false">The Congress shall have the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the several states, and with the Indian Tribes.</content><bbox>[61, 853, 916, 933]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</abstract><content truncated="false">Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</content><bbox>[23, 251, 787, 300]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:2:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</abstract><content truncated="false">>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</content><bbox>[73, 314, 729, 366]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:3:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Removal Era 1830-1850.</abstract><content truncated="false">The Removal Era 1830-1850.</content><bbox>[23, 377, 784, 427]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:4:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Reservation Era 1850-1871.</abstract><content truncated="false">The Reservation Era 1850-1871.</content><bbox>[23, 442, 784, 487]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:5:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Allotments – Assimilation 1887-1934</abstract><content truncated="false">Allotments – Assimilation 1887-1934</content><bbox>[23, 505, 784, 551]</bbox></node>

  <question>Which tables provide information on the recognition of Native American sovereignty through treaties during the Treaty Making Era (1778-1871)?
Select table names from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the table names themselves.</question>

  <evidence_state>
    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:10" type="page" page_index="10"><abstract>Page titled "Tribal Engagement Guidance" from The White House, listing tribal consultation guidance sources including Executive Order 13175 (2000), OMB Implementation Guidance, DHS Tribal Consultation Policy, and CGHQ/District-Level Guidance/Directives. Also lists Congressional/DHS/CG Data Calls such as the Congressional/DHS Tribal Consultation Report, Environmental Justice (EJ) Annual Report, D17 Engagement/Consultation Log, and D17 Strategy/Annual Priorities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:11" type="page" page_index="11"><abstract>Page titled "Consultation and Engagement With Tribes and Alaska Native Organizations" outlines two categories: Formal Consultation with Federally Recognized Tribes, involving letters, meeting logistics, input opportunities, and MOUs; and Engagement with Tribal-Related Stakeholders, including Native Organizations, advocacy groups, ANCSA corporations, and Alaska Native villages.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6" type="page" page_index="6"><abstract>Page outlines Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy, listing eras: Treaty Making Era (1778-1871, >20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty), Removal Era (1830-1850), Reservation Era (1850-1871), Allotments – Assimilation (1887-1934), Termination Era (1953-1968), Self-Determination Era (1968-Present), and Mandate for Federal G-2-G (2000-Present). Includes constitutional quote: “Domestic Dependent Nations.”</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:6:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Termination Era 1953–1968.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:7:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Self-Determination Era 1968-Present</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:8:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Mandate for Federal G-2-G 2000-Present.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:7" type="page" page_index="7"><abstract>Page titled “Tribes in Alaska: Background & Organization” lists key historical events: tribes forced onto reservations in the 1800s, the 1935 Jurisdictional Act and U.S. Supreme Court upholding Tlingit/Haida land claims, Alaska statehood land selection in 1959, and oil discovery in 1969. It also details the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA – 1971), which allocated 44 million acres (about 12%) and $963 million, created 13 for-profit regional corporations, and 12 non-profit corps (approx. 200 village corps), resulting in a unique tribal structure.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:9" type="page" page_index="9"><abstract>Page titled “Native Governance & Consultation: Tribal Consultation is a Mandate – Executive Order 13175 (2000)”. States that in 2000, federal recognition of tribal sovereignty and government-to-government relationship mandated consultation on matters impacting tribal rights, resources, or interests. Notes that in 2003, ANCSA Corps were included in the mandate for consultation. Also includes a quote: “The Congress shall have the power... to regulate commerce with foreign nations, and with the Indian Tribes.”</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:9:block:6:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="9"><abstract>The Congress shall have the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the several states, and with the Indian Tribes.</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="6"><abstract>Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</abstract><content truncated="false">Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</content><bbox>[119, 68, 880, 150]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:10:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Congress shall have the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the several states, and with the Indian Tribes.</abstract><content truncated="false">The Congress shall have the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the several states, and with the Indian Tribes.</content><bbox>[61, 853, 916, 933]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</abstract><content truncated="false">Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</content><bbox>[23, 251, 787, 300]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:2:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</abstract><content truncated="false">>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</content><bbox>[73, 314, 729, 366]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:3:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Removal Era 1830-1850.</abstract><content truncated="false">The Removal Era 1830-1850.</content><bbox>[23, 377, 784, 427]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:4:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Reservation Era 1850-1871.</abstract><content truncated="false">The Reservation Era 1850-1871.</content><bbox>[23, 442, 784, 487]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:5:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Allotments – Assimilation 1887-1934</abstract><content truncated="false">Allotments – Assimilation 1887-1934</content><bbox>[23, 505, 784, 551]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:9:title" type="title" page_index="6"><abstract>“Domestic Dependent Nations”</abstract><content truncated="false">“Domestic Dependent Nations”</content><bbox>[294, 796, 734, 842]</bbox></node>

  <question>Which tables provide information on the recognition of Native American sovereignty through treaties during the Treaty Making Era (1778-1871)?
Select table names from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the table names themselves.</question>

  <evidence_state>
    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:10" type="page" page_index="10"><abstract>Page titled "Tribal Engagement Guidance" from The White House, listing tribal consultation guidance sources including Executive Order 13175 (2000), OMB Implementation Guidance, DHS Tribal Consultation Policy, and CGHQ/District-Level Guidance/Directives. Also lists Congressional/DHS/CG Data Calls such as the Congressional/DHS Tribal Consultation Report, Environmental Justice (EJ) Annual Report, D17 Engagement/Consultation Log, and D17 Strategy/Annual Priorities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:11" type="page" page_index="11"><abstract>Page titled "Consultation and Engagement With Tribes and Alaska Native Organizations" outlines two categories: Formal Consultation with Federally Recognized Tribes, involving letters, meeting logistics, input opportunities, and MOUs; and Engagement with Tribal-Related Stakeholders, including Native Organizations, advocacy groups, ANCSA corporations, and Alaska Native villages.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6" type="page" page_index="6"><abstract>Page outlines Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy, listing eras: Treaty Making Era (1778-1871, >20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty), Removal Era (1830-1850), Reservation Era (1850-1871), Allotments – Assimilation (1887-1934), Termination Era (1953-1968), Self-Determination Era (1968-Present), and Mandate for Federal G-2-G (2000-Present). Includes constitutional quote: “Domestic Dependent Nations.”</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:7" type="page" page_index="7"><abstract>Page titled “Tribes in Alaska: Background & Organization” lists key historical events: tribes forced onto reservations in the 1800s, the 1935 Jurisdictional Act and U.S. Supreme Court upholding Tlingit/Haida land claims, Alaska statehood land selection in 1959, and oil discovery in 1969. It also details the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA – 1971), which allocated 44 million acres (about 12%) and $963 million, created 13 for-profit regional corporations, and 12 non-profit corps (approx. 200 village corps), resulting in a unique tribal structure.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:9" type="page" page_index="9"><abstract>Page titled “Native Governance & Consultation: Tribal Consultation is a Mandate – Executive Order 13175 (2000)”. States that in 2000, federal recognition of tribal sovereignty and government-to-government relationship mandated consultation on matters impacting tribal rights, resources, or interests. Notes that in 2003, ANCSA Corps were included in the mandate for consultation. Also includes a quote: “The Congress shall have the power... to regulate commerce with foreign nations, and with the Indian Tribes.”</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="6"><abstract>Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</abstract><content truncated="false">Federal Indian Policy/Philosophy section heading.</content><bbox>[119, 68, 880, 150]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:10:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Congress shall have the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the several states, and with the Indian Tribes.</abstract><content truncated="false">The Congress shall have the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the several states, and with the Indian Tribes.</content><bbox>[61, 853, 916, 933]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</abstract><content truncated="false">Treaty Making Era 1778-1871.</content><bbox>[23, 251, 787, 300]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:2:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</abstract><content truncated="false">>20 Treaties = Recognition of Sovereignty</content><bbox>[73, 314, 729, 366]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4034595:page:6:block:3:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="6"><abstract>The Removal Era 1830-1850.</abstract><content truncated="false">The Removal Era 1830-1850.</content><bbox>[23, 377, 784, 427]</bbox></node>

question: What were the locations and numbers of reported spiral or corkscrew injuries in seals, excluding Orkney?
detailed_evidences: Page 61 lists the regions and numbers of spiral or corkscrew injuries reported, excluding Orkney: Highland (11), Fife (11), Aberdeenshire (4), Argyll and Bute (7), East Lothian (4), North Ayrshire (2), Moray (1), Shetland (1), and Western Isles (1).

      <node id="4182674:page:60" type="page" page_index="60"><abstract>Section 9 reports 67 seals with spiral or corkscrew trauma, nearly matching 2017’s count, from ten regions including Orkney (23 cases), Highland, and Fife. Most cases involve grey seals (Halichoerus grypus) or harbour seals (Phoca vitulina), with research ongoing to determine if these are due to grey seal predation. Figure 47 shows a harbour seal with typical corkscrew lesions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:60:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="60"><abstract>Section 9: Spiral “corkscrew” trauma seal cases</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:61" type="page" page_index="61"><abstract>Table 6 lists scoring of suspected spiral trauma cases in 2017, detailing SMASS ID, species, date found, region, sex, age group, likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal 'corkscrew' pattern. Cases are from locations including Orkney, East Lothian, and Highland, with species mostly grey seal or harbour seal, and age groups predominantly juvenile.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:62" type="page" page_index="62"><abstract>Page 63 of the Scottish Marine Animal Stranding Scheme 2018 Annual Report. Table lists 16 grey seal strandings from 2016–2018, with species, date found, region, sex, age group, likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal ‘corkscrew’ pattern.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:63" type="page" page_index="63"><abstract>Page 64 of the Scottish Marine Animal Stranding Scheme 2018 Annual Report. Table lists 13 grey seal strandings from 10/12/2018 to 30/12/2018 across Orkney, Shetland, and Fife, with age groups (Unknown, Juvenile, Pup), likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal ‘corkscrew’ pattern.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:63:block:0:table" type="table" page_index="63"><abstract>The table is labeled "simple_table" and contains no visible data, rows, or columns.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:72" type="page" page_index="72"><abstract>A single leatherback turtle (Dermochelys coriacea) was reported entangled in creel lines north northeast of Muldoanich near Barra, Western Isles on August 13, 2018, and released alive. Figure 56 shows the turtle, identified as M445/18, disentangled by fishermen.</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>
      <node id="4182674:page:60:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="60"><abstract>Sixty-seven seals with spiral or corkscrew trauma were reported from ten regions, mostly grey seals (n=46; 70.7%), with 23 from Orkney, 11 each from Highland and Fife, and smaller numbers from other areas; research by SMRU and SMASS continues on whether these injuries result from grey seal predation.</abstract><content truncated="false">Sixty-seven seals with spiral or corkscrew trauma were reported from ten regions, mostly grey seals (n=46; 70.7%), with 23 from Orkney, 11 each from Highland and Fife, and smaller numbers from other areas; research by SMRU and SMASS continues on whether these injuries result from grey seal predation.</content><bbox>[114, 146, 882, 319]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:60:block:2:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="60"><abstract>Table 6 details all pinniped cases considered potential spiral trauma cases, with final two columns describing likelihood of matching the archetypal spiral ‘corkscrew’ lesion and likelihood of grey seal predation, using criteria from section 6.</abstract><content truncated="false">Table 6 details all pinniped cases considered potential spiral trauma cases, with final two columns describing likelihood of matching the archetypal spiral ‘corkscrew’ lesion and likelihood of grey seal predation, using criteria from section 6.</content><bbox>[114, 327, 884, 413]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:60:block:3:list" type="list" page_index="60"><abstract>List of four ranked likelihood categories for interpreting lesion patterns in relation to seal predation, from least to most certain: Unlikely, Possible, Probable, and Definite.</abstract><content truncated="false">List of four ranked likelihood categories for interpreting lesion patterns in relation to seal predation, from least to most certain: Unlikely, Possible, Probable, and Definite.</content><bbox>[144, 422, 878, 545]</bbox></node>

      <node id="4182674:page:61:block:0:table" type="table" page_index="61"><abstract>Table 6 lists 30 suspected spiral trauma cases from 2017, detailing for each case the SMASS ID, species (mostly Grey seal or Harbour Seal), date found (between 07/01/2018 and 15/04/2018), region, sex, age group, likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal 'corkscrew' pattern.</abstract><content truncated="true">Table 6 lists 30 suspected spiral trauma cases from 2017, detailing for each case the SMASS ID, species (mostly Grey seal or Harbour Seal), date found (between 07/01/2018 and 15/04/2018), region, sex, age group, likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal 'corkscrew' pattern.

Figure 47 shows a harbour seal with typical corkscrew lesions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:60:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="60"><abstract>Section 9: Spiral “corkscrew” trauma seal cases</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:61" type="page" page_index="61"><abstract>Table 6 lists scoring of suspected spiral trauma cases in 2017, detailing SMASS ID, species, date found, region, sex, age group, likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal 'corkscrew' pattern. Cases are from locations including Orkney, East Lothian, and Highland, with species mostly grey seal or harbour seal, and age groups predominantly juvenile.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:62" type="page" page_index="62"><abstract>Page 63 of the Scottish Marine Animal Stranding Scheme 2018 Annual Report. Table lists 16 grey seal strandings from 2016–2018, with species, date found, region, sex, age group, likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal ‘corkscrew’ pattern.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:63" type="page" page_index="63"><abstract>Page 64 of the Scottish Marine Animal Stranding Scheme 2018 Annual Report. Table lists 13 grey seal strandings from 10/12/2018 to 30/12/2018 across Orkney, Shetland, and Fife, with age groups (Unknown, Juvenile, Pup), likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal ‘corkscrew’ pattern.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:63:block:0:table" type="table" page_index="63"><abstract>The table is labeled "simple_table" and contains no visible data, rows, or columns.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:72" type="page" page_index="72"><abstract>A single leatherback turtle (Dermochelys coriacea) was reported entangled in creel lines north northeast of Muldoanich near Barra, Western Isles on August 13, 2018, and released alive. Figure 56 shows the turtle, identified as M445/18, disentangled by fishermen.</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>
      <node id="4182674:page:60:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="60"><abstract>Sixty-seven seals with spiral or corkscrew trauma were reported from ten regions, mostly grey seals (n=46; 70.7%), with 23 from Orkney, 11 each from Highland and Fife, and smaller numbers from other areas; research by SMRU and SMASS continues on whether these injuries result from grey seal predation.</abstract><content truncated="false">Sixty-seven seals with spiral or corkscrew trauma were reported from ten regions, mostly grey seals (n=46; 70.7%), with 23 from Orkney, 11 each from Highland and Fife, and smaller numbers from other areas; research by SMRU and SMASS continues on whether these injuries result from grey seal predation.</content><bbox>[114, 146, 882, 319]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:60:block:2:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="60"><abstract>Table 6 details all pinniped cases considered potential spiral trauma cases, with final two columns describing likelihood of matching the archetypal spiral ‘corkscrew’ lesion and likelihood of grey seal predation, using criteria from section 6.</abstract><content truncated="false">Table 6 details all pinniped cases considered potential spiral trauma cases, with final two columns describing likelihood of matching the archetypal spiral ‘corkscrew’ lesion and likelihood of grey seal predation, using criteria from section 6.</content><bbox>[114, 327, 884, 413]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:60:block:3:list" type="list" page_index="60"><abstract>List of four ranked likelihood categories for interpreting lesion patterns in relation to seal predation, from least to most certain: Unlikely, Possible, Probable, and Definite.</abstract><content truncated="false">List of four ranked likelihood categories for interpreting lesion patterns in relation to seal predation, from least to most certain: Unlikely, Possible, Probable, and Definite.</content><bbox>[144, 422, 878, 545]</bbox></node>
      <node id="4182674:page:60:block:4:image" type="image" page_index="60"><abstract>A harbour seal (Phoca vitulina) from Jemimaville, Highland, showing visible corkscrew/spiral predation lesions consistent with grey seal attack, with exposed internal organs and bloodstains on the surface.</abstract><content truncated="false">A harbour seal (Phoca vitulina) from Jemimaville, Highland, showing visible corkscrew/spiral predation lesions consistent with grey seal attack, with exposed internal organs and bloodstains on the surface.

      <node id="4182674:page:61:block:0:table" type="table" page_index="61"><abstract>Table 6 lists 30 suspected spiral trauma cases from 2017, detailing for each case the SMASS ID, species (mostly Grey seal or Harbour Seal), date found (between 07/01/2018 and 15/04/2018), region, sex, age group, likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal 'corkscrew' pattern.</abstract><content truncated="true">Table 6 lists 30 suspected spiral trauma cases from 2017, detailing for each case the SMASS ID, species (mostly Grey seal or Harbour Seal), date found (between 07/01/2018 and 15/04/2018), region, sex, age group, likelihood of grey seal predation, and whether lesions fit the archetypal 'corkscrew' pattern.
